plainMeaning: In the second, ancient shrine I bow to Lord Candraprabha, and in the third — the Kumāra-vihāra temple — I bow to Śrī Pārśva, who fulfils the longings of all who bow to him.
recitation: taha cirabhavaṇo bīe, vande candappahaṁ tao taïe | paṇāya jaṇa pūriāsaṁ, kumaravihārammi siripāsaṁ ||

wordByWord.gloss: likewise, in the same way, and so too
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit taha < Sanskrit tathā ('thus, so'); a continuative particle linking this entry to the salutations of the preceding verse.
wordByWord.notes: Signals that the same act of homage continues from the previous tīrtha.

wordByWord.etymology: cira ('long-standing, ancient') + bhavaṇa (< Sanskrit bhavana, 'abode, mansion, temple'). The -o is the Prakrit masc. nominative ending used loosely in apposition to bīe.
wordByWord.notes: Here characterises the 'second' shrine as the venerable, time-honoured one; bhavaṇa is the temple housing the Jina image.

wordByWord.etymology: Locative singular of Prakrit bīa < Sanskrit dvitīya ('second'); locative case marks the place of worship.
wordByWord.notes: Ordinal counting of shrines on the same sacred hill — paired with taïe ('in the third') below.

wordByWord.gloss: I bow, I salute, I venerate
wordByWord.etymology: First-person singular present of √vand ('to praise, salute'); identical in Prakrit and Sanskrit. The hymn's core verb of homage.
wordByWord.notes: Governs both Candraprabha here and (by carry-over) Śrī Pārśva in the second half.

wordByWord.etymology: Accusative of Prakrit candappaha < Sanskrit Candraprabha ('moon-radiance'): candra ('moon') + prabhā ('lustre'). Doubled -pp- is regular Prakrit assimilation of -dr-/-pr-.
wordByWord.notes: Object of vande; the eighth Jina, iconographically white/moon-coloured with the crescent-moon emblem.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit tao < Sanskrit tataḥ ('from that, then'); a sequencing adverb moving the recitation onward.
wordByWord.notes: Marks the step from the second shrine to the third.

wordByWord.etymology: Locative singular of Prakrit taïa < Sanskrit tṛtīya ('third').
wordByWord.notes: Parallel to bīe; confirms the verse counts successive shrines at one site.

wordByWord.gloss: of those who bow / who pay homage (the reverent)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit paṇaya < Sanskrit praṇata ('bowed down, humbly saluting'), from pra-√nam ('to bow'). Qualifies jaṇa.
wordByWord.notes: Forms the phrase paṇāya-jaṇa, 'the people who bow', i.e. the devotees.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jaṇa < Sanskrit jana ('person, people'). In genitive force here with paṇāya, 'of the bowing folk'.
wordByWord.notes: paṇāya jaṇa together = 'the devotees who prostrate'; their hopes are the āśā fulfilled below.

wordByWord.gloss: fulfiller of [their] hopes; whose desires are made full
wordByWord.etymology: pūria (< Sanskrit pūrita, 'filled, fulfilled', from √pṝ) + āsa (< Sanskrit āśā, 'hope, longing'); a bahuvrīhi in the accusative agreeing with siripāsaṁ — 'him by whom the hopes (of the bowing folk) are fulfilled'.
wordByWord.notes: An epithet of Pārśva, casting the shrine as wish-granting for sincere worshippers.

wordByWord.etymology: Locative singular (Prakrit -mmi < Sanskrit -smin) of kumara-vihāra: kumara (the royal name/title Kumāra, i.e. Kumārapāla) + vihāra ('temple, monastic shrine'). 'The temple [founded by/named] Kumāra'.
wordByWord.notes: 'Kumāravihāra' is the standard name of Jina temples patronised by the Caulukya king Kumārapāla; this one enshrines Pārśva.

wordByWord.etymology: Accusative of siri-pāsa: siri (< Sanskrit śrī, honorific 'venerable, glorious') + pāsa (< Sanskrit Pārśva). The honorific śrī is fused to the name.
wordByWord.notes: Second object of vande, located in the Kumāra-vihāra; the hymn's recurring Pārśva-devotion.

literalTranslation: Likewise, in the second — the ancient shrine — I bow to Candraprabha; then in the third, [I bow to] Śrī Pārśva in the Kumāra-vihāra, the fulfiller of the hopes of the people who bow.
idiomaticTranslation: And so, in the second shrine — the venerable old temple — I bow before Lord Candraprabha; then in the third, the Kumāra-vihāra, I bow to glorious Pārśvanātha, who makes full the longings of every devotee who bends low before him.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This single gāthā gathers two shrines into one breath of homage, continuing the poet's walk across a sacred site begun in the previous verse. The ordinals are the hinge: bīe ('in the second') and taïe ('in the third') tell us the worshipper is moving shrine-to-shrine on one hill or in one town, not jumping between distant tīrthas — a rhythm that lets the hymn pack several Jinas into a compact stanza. The 'second' shrine is singled out as cira-bhavaṇa, the ancient one, lending it the authority of age; there the reciter bows to Candraprabha, the eighth, white-hued Jina. The 'third' is named outright — the Kumāra-vihāra — a temple of the type endowed by the Caulukya king Kumārapāla, and it houses Śrī Pārśva. The verse's devotional heart is the epithet pūriāsaṁ: Pārśva is 'the fulfiller of the hopes of those who bow' (paṇāya-jaṇa). Note how the grammar enacts the teaching — only the paṇāya jaṇa, the ones who actually prostrate, have their āśā made full; the boon follows the bowing. A single vande does double duty across the line, so the two salutations read as one continuous gesture, true to the garland-stringing technique of the whole stavan.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on bhakti as the operative cause of grace: it is the act of bowing (praṇāma) that draws the fruit, expressed in the compound pūrita-āśa. In Jain terms the Jina is vītarāga — passionless, granting no favours by will — so 'fulfilment of hopes' is understood as the meritorious result (puṇya) and inner purification arising in the devotee's own stream from sincere veneration, not as a deity's intervention. Pairing an ancient shrine (cira-bhavaṇa) with a royally founded one (Kumāra-vihāra) also quietly affirms two strands of tīrtha-sanctity honoured throughout the hymn: time-hallowed holiness and the merit of temple-building (jina-bhavana-kāraṇa) by lay patrons.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For today's reciter the verse models attentive, place-by-place devotion: rather than a vague gesture, one bows deliberately at 'the second… then the third', honouring each Jina in turn. Its lesson is that the fruit of worship tracks the sincerity of one's bowing — approach each image as a paṇāya jaṇa, with the body and heart actually bent low, and let the longing one brings be reshaped, through Pārśva's example, into the longing for liberation.

sources.title: Chandraprabha (eighth Tīrthaṅkara)
sources.consultedFor: Identity and iconography of Candraprabha, the 8th Jina named in the verse

notes: Word glosses are secure. The structural reading is that bīe ('second') and taïe ('third') count successive shrines at a single site whose name carries over from the preceding verse (the Suvarṇagiri/Sonāgiri context of v.87, traditionally Jābālipura/Jalor, a known location of a Kumārapāla Kumāravihāra); this site-identification is probable but not fixed by a colophon and other editions may read it differently. cira-bhavaṇo stands in loose apposition to bīe ('the second [shrine], the ancient temple'); an alternative parsing takes cira-bhavaṇa as a near-proper designation of that shrine. pūriāsaṁ is read as a bahuvrīhi epithet of siripāsaṁ ('fulfiller of the bowing folk's hopes'); the genitive force of paṇāya jaṇa is contextual rather than marked. Orthography is preserved verbatim per the source book; mātrās/anusvāras follow the printed Gujarati text.
